spans.content: Microstructure and tensile behavior of Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C low density steel
Xuan Li, Renbo Song⁎ , Naipeng Zhou, Jiajia Li
School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China
A R T I C L E I N F O
A B S T R A C T
Keywords:
Fe-Mn-Al-C steel
Microstructure
Tensile behavior
TRIP effect
The microstructure and tensile behavior of Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C low density steel were investigated in the present
study. The results show that, after being solution treated at
9 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }
for
^ { 1 \mathrm { h } }
, the steel has a duplex microstructure
consisting of ferrite and
austenite, and it exhibits an excellent combination in mechanical properties,
with a tensile strength of
8 4 6 ~ \mathrm { M P a }
and a total elongation of
, which were attributed to significant con-
tribution of TRIP effect and three stages work hardening behavior. During tensile test, the work hardening
behavior can be defined as three-stage characteristics, in which the stage 2(S2) was linked to the TRIP effect. In
S2, with the increase of tensile strain, austenite volume fraction decreased while martensite volume fraction
increased gradually. During this process, some austenite grains, positioned at grain boundaries among multiple
ferrite grains, were the first ones to transform, while, some austenite grains, located between ferrite laths or
embedded in large ferrite grains were often found to undergo rotations. The TRIP effect and the high dislocation
density in ferrite and martensite significantly contributed to the excellent mechanical property. In addition, the
volume fraction of small angle grain boundaries increased greatly, with a value of over
being observed at
the true strain of 0.27. The steel possessed a density of
6 . 9 5 { \mathrm { g } } / { \mathrm { c m } } ^ { 3 }
, which is about
lower than pure iron.
1. Introduction
The Fe-Mn-Al-C system was recently studied as a promising auto-
motive steel because of its low density, high strength and extended
ductility [1,2]. The extraordinary comprehensive performance was
contributed to the microstructural evolution, mechanical performance
as well as the deformation mechanism. It is generally acknowledged
that, the dominant deformation mechanisms of Fe-Mn-Al-C low density
steels can be classified to transformation-induced plasticity (TRIP),
twining-induced plasticity (TWIP) and dislocation gliding (DG) [3–12].
As for medium Mn
, medium Al
Fe-Mn-Al-C steel, a
duplex phase of ferrite
austenite has been obtained due to the ad-
dition of Al, while austenite is utilized as a secondary phase in ferrite
matrix. Thus, the excellent mechanical properties are largely attributed
to the TRIP effect of retained austenite. It has been reported that such
hardening mechanism is determined by reactions with the adjacent
ferrite and mechanical stability [13,14], and the austenite stability was
associated with element composition, grain size, grain morphology and
so on [15,16]. Moreover, the orientation of retained austenite (Schmid
factors) was proved an important factor in determining the mechanical
stability of retained austenite upon deformation, in addition to the
heterogeneity of grain size [11]. Besides, deformation mechanism can
be changed by the increasing of strain rate [12].
From the perspective of med-Mn, med-Al Fe-Mn-Al-C steel, much
research has been focused on the evaluation of alloy design, micro-
structural evolution and process control, emphasizing the improvement
of properties that TRIP effect has bring for it. For example, Li et al. [17]
reported that, because of significant contribution of TRIP effect and
high dislocation density in austenite, Fe-6.1Mn-1.6Al-0.2 C steel can
achieve a tensile strength of
1 0 3 8 \mathrm { M P a }
and a total elongation of
after water quenching from
6 2 5 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }
Park et al. [18] compared the mi-
crostructure and tensile behavior of two duplex low-density steel con-
taining different Mn content, an alloy obtained under a specific heat
treatment condition showed TRIP effect, which yielded excellent me-
chanical properties of a high tensile strength of
> 9 0 0 \mathrm { M P a }
and a high
total elongation of
However, few studies have been carried out to reveal the austenite/
martensite transformation rule retained austenite during the deforma-
tion in Fe-Mn-Al-C TRIP steels [19,20]. In general, types of retained
austenite grains can be divided into three kinds. The first type of aus-
tenite grains were positioned at the ferrite triple edges. The second type
contained twin faults, and the third type was completely embedded in a
single ferrite grain [21].
In this study, the microstructure and tensile behavior of Fe-8Mn-
6Al-0.2C low density steel were investigated with respect to transfor-
mation characteristic and mechanical stability of retained austenite.
Additionally, the work hardening behavior and fracture mechanism
were analyzed here.

Microstructure and tensile behavior of Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C low density steel
Xuan Li, Renbo Song⁎ , Naipeng Zhou, Jiajia Li
School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China
A R T I C L E I N F O
A B S T R A C T
Keywords:
Fe-Mn-Al-C steel
Microstructure
Tensile behavior
TRIP effect
The microstructure and tensile behavior of Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C low density steel were investigated in the present
study. The results show that, after being solution treated at
9 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }
for
^ { 1 \mathrm { h } }
, the steel has a duplex microstructure
consisting of ferrite and
austenite, and it exhibits an excellent combination in mechanical properties,
with a tensile strength of
8 4 6 ~ \mathrm { M P a }
and a total elongation of
, which were attributed to significant con-
tribution of TRIP effect and three stages work hardening behavior. During tensile test, the work hardening
behavior can be defined as three-stage characteristics, in which the stage 2(S2) was linked to the TRIP effect. In
S2, with the increase of tensile strain, austenite volume fraction decreased while martensite volume fraction
increased gradually. During this process, some austenite grains, positioned at grain boundaries among multiple
ferrite grains, were the first ones to transform, while, some austenite grains, located between ferrite laths or
embedded in large ferrite grains were often found to undergo rotations. The TRIP effect and the high dislocation
density in ferrite and martensite significantly contributed to the excellent mechanical property. In addition, the
volume fraction of small angle grain boundaries increased greatly, with a value of over
being observed at
the true strain of 0.27. The steel possessed a density of
6 . 9 5 { \mathrm { g } } / { \mathrm { c m } } ^ { 3 }
, which is about
lower than pure iron.
1. Introduction
The Fe-Mn-Al-C system was recently studied as a promising auto-
motive steel because of its low density, high strength and extended
ductility [1,2]. The extraordinary comprehensive performance was
contributed to the microstructural evolution, mechanical performance
as well as the deformation mechanism. It is generally acknowledged
that, the dominant deformation mechanisms of Fe-Mn-Al-C low density
steels can be classified to transformation-induced plasticity (TRIP),
twining-induced plasticity (TWIP) and dislocation gliding (DG) [3–12].
As for medium Mn
, medium Al
Fe-Mn-Al-C steel, a
duplex phase of ferrite
austenite has been obtained due to the ad-
dition of Al, while austenite is utilized as a secondary phase in ferrite
matrix. Thus, the excellent mechanical properties are largely attributed
to the TRIP effect of retained austenite. It has been reported that such
hardening mechanism is determined by reactions with the adjacent
ferrite and mechanical stability [13,14], and the austenite stability was
associated with element composition, grain size, grain morphology and
so on [15,16]. Moreover, the orientation of retained austenite (Schmid
factors) was proved an important factor in determining the mechanical
stability of retained austenite upon deformation, in addition to the
heterogeneity of grain size [11]. Besides, deformation mechanism can
be changed by the increasing of strain rate [12].
From the perspective of med-Mn, med-Al Fe-Mn-Al-C steel, much
research has been focused on the evaluation of alloy design, micro-
structural evolution and process control, emphasizing the improvement
of properties that TRIP effect has bring for it. For example, Li et al. [17]
reported that, because of significant contribution of TRIP effect and
high dislocation density in austenite, Fe-6.1Mn-1.6Al-0.2 C steel can
achieve a tensile strength of
1 0 3 8 \mathrm { M P a }
and a total elongation of
after water quenching from
6 2 5 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }
Park et al. [18] compared the mi-
crostructure and tensile behavior of two duplex low-density steel con-
taining different Mn content, an alloy obtained under a specific heat
treatment condition showed TRIP effect, which yielded excellent me-
chanical properties of a high tensile strength of
> 9 0 0 \mathrm { M P a }
and a high
total elongation of
However, few studies have been carried out to reveal the austenite/
martensite transformation rule retained austenite during the deforma-
tion in Fe-Mn-Al-C TRIP steels [19,20]. In general, types of retained
austenite grains can be divided into three kinds. The first type of aus-
tenite grains were positioned at the ferrite triple edges. The second type
contained twin faults, and the third type was completely embedded in a
single ferrite grain [21].
In this study, the microstructure and tensile behavior of Fe-8Mn-
6Al-0.2C low density steel were investigated with respect to transfor-
mation characteristic and mechanical stability of retained austenite.
Additionally, the work hardening behavior and fracture mechanism
were analyzed here.

Chemical composition of the investigated alloys

2. Experimental
The chemical composition of the steel used in this study is listed in
Table 1. The steel was casted using a vacuum induction furnace and
heated at
1 1 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }
for
^ { 2 \mathrm { h } }
, and then hot forged to rods with a section
plane of
3 5 \ : \mathrm { m m } \times 7 0 \ : \mathrm { m m } \times 1 0 0 \ : \mathrm { m m }
, which is followed by air cooling
to room temperature. Then, was after being homogenized at
1 2 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }
for
^ { 2 \mathrm { h } }
, the ingot was hot rolled to
3 \mathrm { m m }
thickness steel sheet through 6
passes. The start rolling and finish rolling temperatures were
1 1 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }
and
8 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,
respectively. The hot-rolled steel was subsequently water
quenched to
5 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,
, followed by air cooling to room temperature. Fi-
nally, solution treatment experiments were performed at
for
^ { 1 \mathrm { h } }
with a water quenching followed. For convenience, the speci-
mens being solution treated at 700, 800, 900 and
1 0 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }
are referred
to as H70, H80, H90 and H100, respectively. For tensile tests, speci-
mens with a gauge width of
1 2 . 5 \mathrm { m m }
and a gauge length of
5 0 \mathrm { m m }
were
machined from the solution-treated sheets along the longitudinal di-
rection. The microstructure was analyzed by scanning electron micro-
scopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and electron
back-scatter diffraction (EBSD). Energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS)
was used to investigate the element distribution in austenite and ferrite.
The volume fraction of austenite was determined by X-ray diffraction
(XRD) with
\mathrm { C u K } _ { \mathrm { a } }
radiation, and the direct comparison method was
adopted, in which the integrated intensities of the peaks of
( 2 0 0 ) \alpha
and
( 2 1 1 ) \alpha
are recognized as ferrite, and those of the peaks of
( 2 2 0 ) \gamma
and
( 3 1 1 ) \gamma
are regarded as austenite [22]. The density of the steel was
measured by a densitometry based on the Archimedes Principle. The
density of the present steel is about
6 . 9 5 { \mathrm { g } } / { \mathrm { c m } } ^ { 3 }
, which is about
lower than pure Fe. The C content in austenite was measured by the
XRD method using the following equation [23]:
\alpha _ { \gamma } = 3 . 5 7 8 + 0 . 0 3 3 0 \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { C } } + 0 . 0 0 5 6 \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { A l } } + 0 . 0 0 0 9 5 \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { M n } }
where
\alpha _ { \gamma }
is austenite lattice parameter, in A, and
\Chi _ { \mathrm { C } } , \mathrm { X _ { M n } }
and
\mathbf { X } _ { \mathbf { A l } }
are
concentrations of C, Mn and Al, respectively, in
{ \mathrm { \mathbf { w t } } } \%
. The austenite
lattice parameter
( \alpha _ { \gamma } )
was determined from a d-spacing of
po-
sition.
3. Results
3.1. Microstructure
The microstructure of the hot-rolled steel after being solution
treated at
for
^ { 1 \mathrm { h } }
is shown in Fig. 1. For H70 (shown in
Fig. 1a), nearly all the original austenite phase decomposed to mar-
tensite and spheroidal
\mathrm { M } _ { 5 } \mathrm { C } _ { 2 }
-carbide, with a volume fraction of
being retained.
\mathbf { M } _ { 5 } \mathbf { C } _ { 2 }
-carbide phase widely distributed both at δ-ferrite
grain boundaries and among martensite laths. For H80 (shown in
Fig. 1b),
\mathbf { M } _ { 5 } \mathbf { C } _ { 2 }
-carbide disappeared, martensite has reverse-transformed
to austenite. Large amount of strip ferrite, which is termed as
\alpha
-ferrite
in order to distinguish it from δ-ferrite that is formed at high tem-
perature, was observed. For H90, with the solution temperature in-
creasing to
{ 9 0 0 } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,
, the microstructure of specimen was mainly con-
sisted of
\alpha
-ferrite, δ-ferrite and austenite, as shown in Fig. 1c. Besides,
the morphology of
\alpha
-ferrite changed from strip shape into fine blocky
shape and the volume fraction of austenite increased to
. For
H100, martensite appeared again with only
austenite retained.
The calculated austenite volume fractions for these steels are listed in
Table 3. The phases were distinguished according to the Vickers mi-
crohardness as is presented in Table 2. And
\mathrm { M } _ { 5 } \mathrm { C } _ { 2 }
-carbide was identified
according to EDS.
3.2. Properties and work hardening behavior
After tensile tests, the mechanical performance is shown in Table 3.
In H70, ultimate tensile strength (UTS) was
7 7 8 . 3 \mathrm { M P a }
, while total
elongation (TE) was
. For H80, TE increased apparently but UTS
decreased. In comparison, H90 steel had much higher UTS of
8 4 6 . 4 \mathrm { M P a }
and also an enhanced TE of
. H100 owns the first-rank
UTS of
8 6 0 . 3 \mathrm { M P a }
but the worst TE of
. With the increase of so-
lution temperature, yield strength (YS) decreases gradually, which was
6 4 2 . 1 \mathrm { M P a }
5 8 7 . 8 \mathrm { M P a }
4 7 3 . 6 \mathrm { M P a }
and
3 9 8 . 3 \mathrm { M P a }
, respectively.
True stress-strain curves and the corresponding work hardening
rate-true strain curves are plotted in Fig. 2. As shown in Fig. 2a, there is
no yield point phenomenon occurring under all the solution treatment
conditions. With the increase of solution temperature, both the critical
stress for uniform deformation and the work hardening rate decreased
obviously. However, for H90, true stress increased abruptly at a true
strain of
, and both the UTS and TE were the highest among the
four steels. As shown in Fig. 3b, the steels possesses an obvious three-
stage work hardening (WH) characteristics: in stage 1, WH rate de-
creased rapidly, which was mainly associated with the deformation of
ferrite; In stage 2, the slight decrease of the WH rate is a consequence of
the strong hardening mechanism, such as TRIP or TWIP effect [24]; In
stage 3, the WH rate decreased quickly again, and this was presumably
associated with the deformation localization of ferrite or martensite.
For H90, the formation of mechanical twining were not observed from
SEM analyses. It can be inferred that, possibly, TRIP effect was the main
mechanism for high tensile strength and excellent ductility. In stage 2,
the WH rate increased with strain in the true strain range 0.03–0.15,
because martensitic transformation took place when a critical stress was
attained. After true strain of 0.15, the WH rate began to decrease
gently, which was the consequence of a potential competition between
the increased stress and stress relaxation induced by the TRIP effect.
3.3. Tensile behavior and austenite stability
In order to observe the TRIP effect, the EBSD analysis was carried
out on H90 under different true strains of 0.03, 0.10, 0.20 and, 0.27,
respectively. Fig. 3 shows the superimposed maps of phase distribution
map and band contrast map, which can be used not only to distinguish
ferrite and austenite grains from martensite grains, but also to measure
the volume fractions of austenite. Martensite grains usually show a poor
band contrast because of their high dislocation density, indicated in
black. Austenite was marked in green and ferrite was marked in gray. At
various strain stages, the calculated austenite volume fractions were
and
, respectively. It can be concluded that
deformation induced martensite transformation occurred continuously
with the increase of true strain, which was consistent with the above
inference. Both blocky and lath-like retained austenite grains were
observed at a true strain of 0.03. Blocky retained austenite was mainly
located at the triple junction boundaries, as shown in square region,
while lath-like retained austenite was always distributed between fer-
rite laths or embedded in a single ferrite grain, as shown in ellipse re-
gion. Along with the deformation, blocky austenite grains disappeared
gradually and only a few were retained at a true strain of 0.20. How-
ever, at a true strain of 0.27, almost the whole retained austenite was
lath-like austenite. In addition, a large portion of small angle grain
boundaries (green line) yielding to
was observed at this strain rate,
while the values of those under other strains (0.03, 0.10 and 0.20 in
turn) were
, respectively. It has been reported that
abundant small angle grain boundaries can significantly improve the
deformation ability of the material [25].
Besides, the orientation of retained austenite relative to the loading
direction (Schmid factors) was also investigated, and the Schmid factor
maps are presented in Fig. 4. The Schmid factor were always used to

3.3. Tensile behavior and austenite stability
In order to observe the TRIP effect, the EBSD analysis was carried
out on H90 under different true strains of 0.03, 0.10, 0.20 and, 0.27,
respectively. Fig. 3 shows the superimposed maps of phase distribution
map and band contrast map, which can be used not only to distinguish
ferrite and austenite grains from martensite grains, but also to measure
the volume fractions of austenite. Martensite grains usually show a poor
band contrast because of their high dislocation density, indicated in
black. Austenite was marked in green and ferrite was marked in gray. At
various strain stages, the calculated austenite volume fractions were
and
, respectively. It can be concluded that
deformation induced martensite transformation occurred continuously
with the increase of true strain, which was consistent with the above
inference. Both blocky and lath-like retained austenite grains were
observed at a true strain of 0.03. Blocky retained austenite was mainly
located at the triple junction boundaries, as shown in square region,
while lath-like retained austenite was always distributed between fer-
rite laths or embedded in a single ferrite grain, as shown in ellipse re-
gion. Along with the deformation, blocky austenite grains disappeared
gradually and only a few were retained at a true strain of 0.20. How-
ever, at a true strain of 0.27, almost the whole retained austenite was
lath-like austenite. In addition, a large portion of small angle grain
boundaries (green line) yielding to
was observed at this strain rate,
while the values of those under other strains (0.03, 0.10 and 0.20 in
turn) were
, respectively. It has been reported that
abundant small angle grain boundaries can significantly improve the
deformation ability of the material [25].
Besides, the orientation of retained austenite relative to the loading
direction (Schmid factors) was also investigated, and the Schmid factor
maps are presented in Fig. 4. The Schmid factor were always used to
measure the relative ease with each grain slips. It is well known that
grains with the higher Schmid factor will undergo deformation faster
because the slip planes in these grains do not need to undergo much
rotation before the resolved shear stress reaches a critical value [26]. In
this paper, the grains with a high Schmid factor were indicated in red,
whereas those with a very low Schmid factor were indicated in blue
using rainbow coloring. As shown in Fig. 4a, the relative volume frac-

This means retained austenite possessing relatively small Schmid fac-
tors tends to remain untransformed during tensile deformation. This
tendency is clearly depicted in Fig. 4e.
3.4. Fracture mechanism
Fig. 5a shows the fracture morphology of H90 steel, which possesses
the common features of quasi-cleavage fracture under different solution
treatment conditions. There were some flat "class cleavage" facets, mi-
cropores, tear edges, secondary cracks and a little dimples. The river
pattern was short and curved with less tributaries, and the herringbone
crack showed the direction of crack propagation. Generally, the failure
mode of quasi-cleavage fracture is thought to be a mixed fracture
characteristics, in which the ductile fracture area is mainly deduced
from austenite deformation and that the quasi-fracture area is mainly
correlated with ferrite deformation [27]. Fig. 5b and c present typical
TEM microsgraphs of H90 in the vicinity of fracture area. It is obvious
that a small amount of austenite was still remaining after the strong
TRIP effect during tensile deformation. Moreover, high density dis-
location were observed in both ferrite and martensite, which could play
an active role in enhancing the mechanical property.
4. Discussions

perature, which is definitely related to the microstructural evolution.
Due to the Al addition, a duplex microstructure of δ-ferrite and auste-

3.4. Fracture mechanism
Fig. 5a shows the fracture morphology of H90 steel, which possesses
the common features of quasi-cleavage fracture under different solution
treatment conditions. There were some flat "class cleavage" facets, mi-
cropores, tear edges, secondary cracks and a little dimples. The river
pattern was short and curved with less tributaries, and the herringbone

TRIP effect during tensile deformation. Moreover, high density dis-
location were observed in both ferrite and martensite, which could play

4. Discussions
4.1. The relationship between microstructure and mechanical performance
The mechanical performance varied with different solution tem-
perature, which is definitely related to the microstructural evolution.
Due to the Al addition, a duplex microstructure of δ-ferrite and auste-
nite was obtained at high temperature. Some δ-ferrite phase was
retained during the subsequent solution treatment process, while aus-
tenite transformed to different phases under various conditions. For
H70, UTS was high attributed to the secondary phase and martensite
strengthening, while TE was low because of low austenite fraction. For
H80, with the austenite volume fraction increasing to
, TE in-
creased apparently but UTS decreased. For H90, the excellent combi-
nation of UTS and TE was obtained not only attributed to the most
austenite fraction with higher hardenability than that of ferrite but
uniform microstructure. As is seen, banded ferrite grains had been
crushed and separated, austenite grains kept equiaxed and the uni-
formity was enhanced dramatically. Furthermore, good coordination of
austenite and ferrite during deformation was another vital factor to
improve mechanical performance [28]. For H100, the best UTS was
attributed to the appearing of martensite while lowest austenite volume
fraction led to the worst TE. With the increase of solution temperature,
YS decreases gradually. On the one hand, plastic deformation generally
begins with the softest phase, higher austenite volume fraction will
induce plastic deformation easily, and high solution temperature forces
the softening of microstructure, leading to yield more prone to occur.
On the other hand, it can be explained by asymmetric distribution of
ferrite and coarsening of the grains due to the Hall–Petch relation.

4.2. The deformation mechanism in association with austenite stability
Two types of austenite grains were carefully analyzed in the present
work. Some austenite grains were blocky with a high Schmid factor,
and were positioned at the ferrite triple edges. The other ones were
lath-like and were located between ferrite laths or embedded in a single
ferrite grain with a lower Schmid factor. Fig. 6 shows a schematic il-
lustration of martensitic transformation during tensile deformation for
the experimental steel, in which martensite were marked in blue, ferrite
delineated in gray, blocky austenite in white and lath-like austenite in
red. With the increase of tensile strain, the blocky austenite took the
initiative to transform into martensite, which can be attributed to the

4.2. The deformation mechanism in association with austenite stability
Two types of austenite grains were carefully analyzed in the present
work. Some austenite grains were blocky with a high Schmid factor,
and were positioned at the ferrite triple edges. The other ones were
lath-like and were located between ferrite laths or embedded in a single
ferrite grain with a lower Schmid factor. Fig. 6 shows a schematic il-
lustration of martensitic transformation during tensile deformation for
the experimental steel, in which martensite were marked in blue, ferrite
delineated in gray, blocky austenite in white and lath-like austenite in
red. With the increase of tensile strain, the blocky austenite took the
initiative to transform into martensite, which can be attributed to the
higher localized stress. It has been accepted that there exist high free
energy and atomic activity at grain boundaries and phase boundaries.
Consequently, a triaxial state of stress and more strain can be favored
on these blocky austenite grains during deformation. In this case,
martensitic transformation is prone to take place at ferrite triple junc-
tion boundaries [29]. However, for lath-like austenite grains with lower
Schmid factor, they would undergo rotations firstly during deformation,
which were dependent on the crystallographic orientation of the grains
with respect to the straining direction.
Fig. 7. (a) The XRD profiles and (b) the corresponding austenite fractions for the steels before and after tensile deformation. Note that, in b, the martensitic transformation ratios for the
three steels are also plotted. (F - after fraction).
higher localized stress. It has been accepted that there exist high free
energy and atomic activity at grain boundaries and phase boundaries.
Consequently, a triaxial state of stress and more strain can be favored
on these blocky austenite grains during deformation. In this case,
martensitic transformation is prone to take place at ferrite triple junc-
tion boundaries [29]. However, for lath-like austenite grains with lower
Schmid factor, they would undergo rotations firstly during deformation,
which were dependent on the crystallographic orientation of the grains
with respect to the straining direction.
From the perspective of dislocation glide, both the austenite and
ferrite were deformed by the dislocation slip at the beginning of de-
formation. Next, martensite was independently formed inside austenite
grain, not only acting as a barrier for dislocation glide but also as a site
for dislocation accumulation and as a source of partial dislocations. As
the deformation continues, dislocation multiplied constantly in mar-
tensite, while wavy glide in ferrite leading to the formation of com-
plicated dislocation structure. This is why high density dislocation were
observed in both ferrite and martensite, as shown in Fig. 5b and c. Thus,
the dynamic recovery and dislocation climbing were restricted, and the
strength performance were enhanced eventually [30–33].
Austenite stability among H70~H90 steel was discussed here,
which can be explained by the difference in chemical composition of
austenite. Table 4 shows the contents of elements of austenite and

5. Conclusions
In the present study, the tensile behavior of Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C low
density steel was investigated in relation to microstructural evolution
and deformation mechanisms, showing the operation of TRIP effect.
(1) The hot-rolled steel solution treated at
9 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }
for
^ { 1 \mathrm { h } }
, possessing a
dual-phase microstructure consisting of ferrite and over
aus-
tenite, exhibited best combination of mechanical properties, and
was characterized by excellent combination of UTS of
8 3 6 . 4 \mathrm { M P a }
YS of

, and TE of
(2) The TRIP effect and the high dislocation density in ferrite and
martensite significantly contributed to the excellent combination in
mechanical performance. The considerable C and Mn enrichment in
austenite, as well as its high volume fraction, enables the me-
tastable austenite to transform into martensite during tensile de-
formation. Because of the variation in element partitioning, the
martensitic transformation ratio increased with the solution tem-
perature in the range
. Moreover,
\mathbf { M } _ { s }
temperature was
another factor influencing the austenite stability.
(3) During tensile test, the steel exhibited an obvious three-stage work
hardening characteristics, in which the S2 was linked to the TRIP
effect. With the increase of tensile strain, the austenite volume
fraction decreased while martensite volume fraction increased
gradually. The austenite grains positioned at grain boundaries be-
tween multiple ferrite grains took the initiative to transform,
whereas some other austenite grains, which were located in ferrite
laths or embedded in larger ferrite grains, were often found to
undergo rotations. In addition, the volume fraction of small angle
grain boundaries increased greatly, with a value of over
being
observed at the true strain of 0.27.
